29 May 1630 ([N.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates"): 8 June 1630) [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace"), Westminster, England

6 February 1685 (aged 54) ([N.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates"): 16 February 1685) [Whitehall Palace](/wiki/Whitehall_Palace "Whitehall Palace"), Westminster, England
[Charles I of England](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England")
[Henrietta Maria of France](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France")

(29 May 1630 – 6 February 1685)[[c]](#cite_note-3) was [King of Scotland](/wiki/King_of_Scotland "King of Scotland") from 1649 until 1651 and [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England"), Scotland, and [Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland") from the [1660 Restoration](/wiki/1660_Restoration "1660 Restoration") of the monarchy until his death in 1685.

**Charles II** (29 May 1630 – 6 February 1685)[[c]](#cite_note-3) was [King of Scotland](/wiki/King_of_Scotland "King of Scotland") from 1649 until 1651 and [King of England](/wiki/King_of_England "King of England"), Scotland, and [Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland") from the [1660 Restoration](/wiki/1660_Restoration "1660 Restoration") of the monarchy until his death in 1685.

Charles II was the eldest surviving child of [Charles I of England, Scotland and Ireland](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England,_Scotland_and_Ireland "Charles I of England, Scotland and Ireland") and [Henrietta Maria of France](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France"). After [Charles I's execution](/wiki/Charles_I%27s_execution "Charles I's execution") at [Whitehall](/wiki/Palace_of_Whitehall "Palace of Whitehall") on 30 January 1649, at the climax of the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") proclaimed Charles II king on 5 February 1649. However, England entered the period known as the [English Interregnum](/wiki/English_Interregnum "English Interregnum") or the [English Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Commonwealth "English Commonwealth") with a republican government eventually led by [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"). Cromwell defeated Charles II at the [Battle of Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") on 3 September 1651, and Charles [fled to mainland Europe](/wiki/Escape_of_Charles_II "Escape of Charles II"). Cromwell became [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector "Lord Protector") of England, Scotland and Ireland. Charles spent the next nine years in exile in France, the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") and the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"). A political crisis after Cromwell's death in 1658 resulted in the [restoration of the monarchy](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") in 1660, and Charles was invited to return to Britain. On 29 May 1660, his 30th birthday, he was received in London to public acclaim.

[Charles's English Parliament](/wiki/Cavalier_Parliament "Cavalier Parliament") enacted the [Clarendon Code](/wiki/Clarendon_Code "Clarendon Code"), to shore up the position of the [re-established](/wiki/Established_Church "Established Church") [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Charles acquiesced to these new laws even though he favoured a policy of [religious tolerance](/wiki/Religious_tolerance "Religious tolerance"). The major foreign policy issue of his early reign was the [Second Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Dutch_War "Second Anglo-Dutch War"). In 1670, he entered into the [Treaty of Dover](/wiki/Treaty_of_Dover "Treaty of Dover"), an alliance with his cousin, King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France. Louis agreed to aid him in the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War") and pay him a pension, and Charles secretly promised to convert to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") at an unspecified future date. Charles attempted to introduce [religious freedom](/wiki/Religious_freedom "Religious freedom") for Catholics and Protestant [dissenters](/wiki/Dissenter "Dissenter") with his [1672 Royal Declaration of Indulgence](/wiki/1672_Royal_Declaration_of_Indulgence "1672 Royal Declaration of Indulgence"), but the [English Parliament](/wiki/English_Parliament "English Parliament") forced him to withdraw it. In 1679, [Titus Oates](/wiki/Titus_Oates "Titus Oates")'s fabrication of a supposed [Popish Plot](/wiki/Popish_Plot "Popish Plot") sparked the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis") when it was revealed that Charles's brother and [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive"), [James, Duke of York](/wiki/James,_Duke_of_York "James, Duke of York"), had become a Catholic. The crisis saw the birth of the pro-exclusion [Whig](/wiki/British_Whig_Party "British Whig Party") and anti-exclusion [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") parties. Charles sided with the Tories and, after the discovery of the [Rye House Plot](/wiki/Rye_House_Plot "Rye House Plot") to murder Charles and James in 1683, some Whig leaders were executed or forced into exile. Charles dissolved the English Parliament in 1681 and ruled alone until his death in 1685.

A patron of the arts and sciences, Charles became known for his affability and friendliness, and for allowing his subjects easy access to his person. But he also showed an almost impenetrable reserve, especially concerning his political agendas. His court gained a reputation for moral laxity.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979361–363-4) Charles's marriage to [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") produced no surviving children, but the king acknowledged at least 12 illegitimate children by various mistresses. He was succeeded by his brother James.

## Early life, civil war and exile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life, civil war and exile")]

[![Baby in white christening robe](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Charles_II_Prince_of_Wales_Egmont.jpg/250px-Charles_II_Prince_of_Wales_Egmont.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_Prince_of_Wales_Egmont.jpg)

Charles as an infant in 1630, painting attributed to [Justus van Egmont](/wiki/Justus_van_Egmont "Justus van Egmont")

Charles was born on 29 May 1630 at [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace"). He was the eldest surviving son of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), king of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), and his wife [Henrietta Maria of France](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France"), sister of King [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII"). Charles was their second child (the first being a son born about a year before, who had died within a day).[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir1996255–257-5) He was baptised on 27 June in the [Chapel Royal](/wiki/Chapel_Royal "Chapel Royal") by [William Laud](/wiki/William_Laud "William Laud"), a future [archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), and during his infancy was supervised by the Protestant [Countess of Dorset](/wiki/Edward_Sackville,_4th_Earl_of_Dorset#Family "Edward Sackville, 4th Earl of Dorset"). His godparents included his maternal uncle Louis XIII and maternal grandmother, [Marie de' Medici](/wiki/Marie_de%27_Medici "Marie de' Medici"), the Dowager Queen of France, both of whom were Catholics.[[3]](#cite_note-6) At birth, Charles automatically became [Duke of Cornwall](/wiki/Duke_of_Cornwall "Duke of Cornwall") and [Duke of Rothesay](/wiki/Duke_of_Rothesay "Duke of Rothesay"), and the possessor of several other associated titles. At or around his eighth birthday, he was designated [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales"), though he was never formally invested.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir1996255–257-5)

In August 1642, the long-running dispute between Charles I and [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") culminated in the outbreak of the [First English Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War"). In October, Prince Charles and his younger brother [James](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") were present at the [Battle of Edgehill](/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill "Battle of Edgehill") and spent the next two years based in the [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") capital of [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"). In January 1645, Charles was given his own Council and made titular head of Royalist forces in the [West Country](/wiki/West_Country "West Country").[[4]](#cite_note-7) By spring 1646, most of the region had been occupied by [Parliamentarian](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead") forces and Charles went into exile to avoid capture. From [Falmouth](/wiki/Falmouth,_Cornwall "Falmouth, Cornwall"), he went first to the [Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Isles_of_Scilly "Isles of Scilly"), then to [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"), and finally to France, where his mother was already living under the protection of his first cousin, the eight-year-old [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV").[[5]](#cite_note-8) Charles I surrendered into captivity in May 1646.

During the [Second English Civil War](/wiki/Second_English_Civil_War "Second English Civil War") in 1648, Charles moved to [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), where his sister [Mary](/wiki/Mary,_Princess_Royal_and_Princess_of_Orange "Mary, Princess Royal and Princess of Orange") and his brother-in-law [William II, Prince of Orange](/wiki/William_II,_Prince_of_Orange "William II, Prince of Orange"), seemed more likely to provide substantial aid to the Royalist cause than his mother's French relations.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser197955–56-9) Although part of the Parliamentarian fleet defected, it did not reach Scotland in time to join up with the Royalist [Engager](/wiki/Engager "Engager") army led by the [Duke of Hamilton](/wiki/James_Hamilton,_1st_Duke_of_Hamilton "James Hamilton, 1st Duke of Hamilton") before it was defeated at [Preston](/wiki/Battle_of_Preston_(1648) "Battle of Preston (1648)") by the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser197957–60-10)

[![Charles as a boy with shoulder-length black hair and standing in a martial pose](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/William_Dobson_-_Charles_II%2C_1630_-_1685._King_of_Scots_1649_-_1685._King_of_England_and_Ireland_1660_-_1685_%28When_Prince_of_Wales%2C_with_a_page%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Dobson_-_Charles_II,_1630_-_1685._King_of_Scots_1649_-_1685._King_of_England_and_Ireland_1660_-_1685_(When_Prince_of_Wales,_with_a_page)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Portrait by [William Dobson](/wiki/William_Dobson "William Dobson"), c. 1642 or 1643

At The Hague, Charles had a brief affair with [Lucy Walter](/wiki/Lucy_Walter "Lucy Walter"), who later falsely claimed that they had secretly married.[[8]](#cite_note-11) Her son, [James Crofts](/wiki/James_Scott,_1st_Duke_of_Monmouth "James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth") (afterwards [Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/Duke_of_Monmouth "Duke of Monmouth") and [Duke of Buccleuch](/wiki/Duke_of_Buccleuch "Duke of Buccleuch")), was one of Charles's many illegitimate children who became prominent in British society.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir1996255–257-5) Despite his son's diplomatic efforts to save him, [Charles I was executed](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") in January 1649, and England became a [republic](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"). On 5 February, the [Covenanter](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter") [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") proclaimed Charles II as "King of Great Britain, France and Ireland" at the [Mercat Cross, Edinburgh](/wiki/Mercat_Cross,_Edinburgh "Mercat Cross, Edinburgh"),[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERPS1649/1/71-12) but refused to allow him to enter Scotland unless he agreed to establish [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") as the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") in all three of his kingdoms.

When negotiations with the Scots stalled, Charles authorised [Lord Montrose](/wiki/James_Graham,_1st_Marquess_of_Montrose "James Graham, 1st Marquess of Montrose") to land in the [Orkney Islands](/wiki/Orkney_Islands "Orkney Islands") with a small army to threaten the Scots with invasion, in the hope of forcing an agreement more to his liking. Montrose feared that Charles would accept a compromise, and so chose to invade mainland Scotland anyway. He was captured and executed. Charles reluctantly promised that he would abide by the terms of a [treaty agreed between him and the Scots Parliament](/wiki/Treaty_of_Breda_(1650) "Treaty of Breda (1650)") at [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda"), and support the [Solemn League and Covenant](/wiki/Solemn_League_and_Covenant "Solemn League and Covenant"), which authorised [Presbyterian church governance](/wiki/Presbyterian_church_governance "Presbyterian church governance") across Britain. Upon his arrival in Scotland on 23 June 1650, he formally agreed to the Covenant; his abandonment of [Episcopal](/wiki/Episcopy "Episcopy") church governance, although winning him support in Scotland, left him unpopular in England. Charles himself soon came to despise the "villainy" and "hypocrisy" of the Covenanters.[[10]](#cite_note-13) Charles was provided with a Scottish court, and the record of his [food and household expenses](/wiki/Food_and_the_Scottish_royal_household "Food and the Scottish royal household") at [Falkland Palace](/wiki/Falkland_Palace "Falkland Palace") and [Perth](/wiki/Perth,_Scotland "Perth, Scotland") survives.[[11]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Cast_gold_medal_of_Charles_II_Stuart.jpg/250px-Cast_gold_medal_of_Charles_II_Stuart.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cast_gold_medal_of_Charles_II_Stuart.jpg)

Cast gold coronation medal of Charles II, dated 1651

Charles's alliance with the Scots led to the [Anglo-Scottish War](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_war_(1650%E2%80%931652) "Anglo-Scottish war (1650–1652)") of 1650 to 1652. On 3 September 1650, the Covenanters were defeated at [Dunbar](/wiki/Battle_of_Dunbar_(1650) "Battle of Dunbar (1650)") by a much smaller force commanded by [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"). The Scots were divided between moderate Engagers and the more radical [Kirk Party](/wiki/Kirk_Party "Kirk Party"), who even fought each other. Disillusioned by these divisions, Charles rode north to join an Engager force in October, an event which became known as "the Start", but within two days members of the Kirk Party had recovered him.[[12]](#cite_note-15) Nevertheless, the Scots remained Charles's best hope of restoration, and he was [crowned King of Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_coronation_of_Charles_II "Scottish coronation of Charles II") at [Scone Abbey](/wiki/Scone_Abbey "Scone Abbey") on 1 January 1651. With Cromwell's forces threatening Charles's position in Scotland, it was decided to mount an attack on England, but many of their most experienced soldiers had been excluded on religious grounds by the Kirk Party, whose leaders also refused to participate, among them [Lord Argyll](/wiki/Archibald_Campbell,_1st_Marquess_of_Argyll "Archibald Campbell, 1st Marquess of Argyll"). Opposition to what was primarily a Scottish army meant few English Royalists joined as it moved south, and the invasion ended in defeat at the [Battle of Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") on 3 September 1651. [Charles managed to escape](/wiki/Escape_of_Charles_II "Escape of Charles II") and landed in [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy") six weeks later on 16 October, even though there was a reward of £1,000 on his head, anyone caught helping him was at risk of being put to death, and he was difficult to disguise, being over 6 ft (1.8 m), which was unusually tall for the time.[[13]](#cite_note-16)[[d]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Charles_II_%28de_Champaigne%29.jpg/250px-Charles_II_%28de_Champaigne%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_(de_Champaigne).jpg)

Charles in exile, painted by [Philippe de Champaigne](/wiki/Philippe_de_Champaigne "Philippe de Champaigne"), c. 1653

Under the [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government "Instrument of Government") passed by Parliament, Cromwell was appointed [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector#Cromwellian_Commonwealth "Lord Protector") of England, Scotland and Ireland in 1653, effectively placing the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") under military rule. Charles lived a life of leisure at [Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Saint-Germain-en-Laye "Saint-Germain-en-Laye") near Paris,[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus197254-19) living on a grant from Louis XIV of 600 [livres](/wiki/French_livre "French livre") a month.[[16]](#cite_note-20) Charles could not obtain sufficient finance or support to mount a serious challenge to Cromwell's government. Despite the [Stuart family](/wiki/Stuart_family "Stuart family") connections through Henrietta Maria and the Princess of Orange, France and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") allied themselves with Cromwell's government from 1654, forcing Charles to leave France and turn to Spain for aid, which at that time ruled the [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton198974–112-21)

Charles made the [Treaty of Brussels](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brussels_(1656) "Treaty of Brussels (1656)") with Spain in 1656. This gathered Spanish support for a restoration in return for Charles's contribution to the war against France. Charles raised a ragtag army from his exiled subjects; this small, underpaid, poorly equipped and ill-disciplined force formed the nucleus of the post-Restoration army.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979156–157-22) The Commonwealth made the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1657) "Treaty of Paris (1657)") with France in 1657 to join them in war against Spain in the Netherlands. Royalist supporters in the Spanish force were led by Charles's younger brother [James, Duke of York](/wiki/James,_Duke_of_York "James, Duke of York").[[19]](#cite_note-23) At the [Battle of the Dunes](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Dunes_(1658) "Battle of the Dunes (1658)") in 1658, as part of the larger Spanish force, Charles's army of around 2,000 clashed with Commonwealth troops fighting with the French. By the end of the battle Charles's force was about 1,000 and with Dunkirk given to the English the prospect of a Royalist expedition to England was dashed.[[20]](#cite_note-24)

## Restoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Restoration")]

Further information: [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration")

After Oliver Cromwell's death in 1658, Charles's initial chances of regaining the Crown seemed slim; Cromwell was succeeded as Lord Protector by his son [Richard](/wiki/Richard_Cromwell "Richard Cromwell") but the new Lord Protector had little experience of either military or civil administration. In 1659, the [Rump Parliament](/wiki/Rump_Parliament "Rump Parliament") was recalled and Richard Cromwell resigned. During the civil and military unrest that followed, [George Monck](/wiki/George_Monck "George Monck"), the Governor of Scotland, was concerned that the nation would descend into anarchy.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979160–165-25) Monck and his army marched into the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London"), and forced the Rump Parliament to readmit members of the [Long Parliament](/wiki/Long_Parliament "Long Parliament") who had been excluded in December 1648, during [Pride's Purge](/wiki/Pride%27s_Purge "Pride's Purge"). Parliament dissolved itself, and there was a general election for the first time in almost 20 years.[[22]](#cite_note-26) The outgoing Parliament defined the electoral qualifications intending to bring about the return of a Presbyterian majority.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller199124–25-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Ball_given_to_Charles_II_at_the_Hague_on_his_departure_to_England.jpg/250px-Ball_given_to_Charles_II_at_the_Hague_on_his_departure_to_England.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ball_given_to_Charles_II_at_the_Hague_on_his_departure_to_England.jpg)

Ball given to Charles at [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") on his departure to England

The restrictions against royalist candidates and voters were widely ignored, and the elections resulted in a [House of Commons](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") that was fairly evenly divided on political grounds between Royalists and Parliamentarians and on religious grounds between [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans") and Presbyterians.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller199124–25-27) The so-called [Convention Parliament](/wiki/Convention_Parliament_(1660) "Convention Parliament (1660)") assembled on 25 April 1660, and soon afterwards welcomed the [Declaration of Breda](/wiki/Declaration_of_Breda "Declaration of Breda"), in which Charles promised lenience and tolerance. There would be liberty of conscience, and Anglican church policy would not be harsh. He would not exile past enemies nor confiscate their wealth. There would be pardons for nearly all his opponents except the [regicides](/wiki/Regicides "Regicides"). Above all, Charles promised to rule in cooperation with Parliament.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaley19855-28) The English Parliament resolved to proclaim Charles king and invite him to return, a message that reached Charles at [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda") on 8 May 1660.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989131-29) In Ireland, a [convention](/wiki/Irish_Convention_(1660) "Irish Convention (1660)") had been called earlier in the year and had already declared for Charles. On 14 May, he was proclaimed king in Dublin.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeaward2004-30)

[![Seascape of vessels along a low-lying coastline](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/The_arrival_of_King_Charles_II_of_England_in_Rotterdam%2C_may_24_1660_%28Lieve_Pietersz._Verschuier%2C_1665%29.jpg/250px-The_arrival_of_King_Charles_II_of_England_in_Rotterdam%2C_may_24_1660_%28Lieve_Pietersz._Verschuier%2C_1665%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_arrival_of_King_Charles_II_of_England_in_Rotterdam,_may_24_1660_(Lieve_Pietersz._Verschuier,_1665).jpg)

Charles sailed from his exile in the Netherlands to his restoration in England in May 1660. Painting by [Lieve Verschuier](/wiki/Lieve_Verschuier "Lieve Verschuier").

Charles set out for England from [Scheveningen](/wiki/Scheveningen "Scheveningen"), arrived in [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover") on 25 May 1660 and reached London on 29 May, his 30th birthday. Although Charles and Parliament granted amnesty to nearly all of Cromwell's supporters in the [Act of Indemnity and Oblivion](/wiki/Act_of_Indemnity_and_Oblivion "Act of Indemnity and Oblivion"), 50 people were specifically excluded.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979190-31) In the end nine of the [regicides](/wiki/List_of_regicides_of_Charles_I "List of regicides of Charles I") were executed:[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Royal_Household2009-32) they were [hanged, drawn and quartered](/wiki/Hanged,_drawn_and_quartered "Hanged, drawn and quartered"), whereas others were given life imprisonment or excluded from office for life. The bodies of Oliver Cromwell, [Henry Ireton](/wiki/Henry_Ireton "Henry Ireton") and [John Bradshaw](/wiki/John_Bradshaw_(Judge) "John Bradshaw (Judge)") were subjected to [posthumous decapitations](/wiki/Posthumous_execution "Posthumous execution").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979185-33)

The English Parliament granted Charles an annual income to run the government of £1.2 million,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus197294-34) generated largely from customs and excise duties. The grant, however, proved to be insufficient for most of Charles's reign. For the most part, the actual revenue was much lower, which led to attempts to economise at court by reducing the size and expenses of the [royal household](/wiki/Royal_household "Royal household")[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus197294-34) and raising money through unpopular innovations such as the [hearth tax](/wiki/Hearth_tax "Hearth tax").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeaward2004-30)

In the latter half of 1660, Charles's joy at the Restoration was tempered by the deaths of his siblings [Henry](/wiki/Henry_Stuart,_Duke_of_Gloucester "Henry Stuart, Duke of Gloucester") and Mary of [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"). At around the same time, [Anne Hyde](/wiki/Anne_Hyde "Anne Hyde"), the daughter of Lord Chancellor [Edward Hyde](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon"), revealed that she was pregnant by Charles's brother James, whom she had secretly married. Edward Hyde, who had not known of either the marriage or the pregnancy, was created [Earl of Clarendon](/wiki/Earl_of_Clarendon "Earl of Clarendon") and his position as Charles's favourite minister was strengthened.[[31]](#cite_note-35)

### Clarendon Code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Clarendon Code")]

[![Charles wearing a crown and ermine-lined robe](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Charles_II_by_John_Michael_Wright.jpg/250px-Charles_II_by_John_Michael_Wright.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_by_John_Michael_Wright.jpg)

Coronation portrait: Charles was crowned at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") on 23 April 1661.[[32]](#cite_note-36)

The Convention Parliament was dissolved in December 1660, and, shortly after Charles's [English coronation](/wiki/Coronation_of_Charles_II_of_England "Coronation of Charles II of England"), the second English Parliament of the reign assembled. Dubbed the [Cavalier Parliament](/wiki/Cavalier_Parliament "Cavalier Parliament"), it was overwhelmingly Royalist and Anglican. It sought to discourage [non-conformity](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") to the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and passed several acts to secure Anglican dominance. The [Corporation Act 1661](/wiki/Corporation_Act_1661 "Corporation Act 1661") required municipal officeholders to swear allegiance;[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989169-37) the [Act of Uniformity 1662](/wiki/Act_of_Uniformity_1662 "Act of Uniformity 1662") made the use of the [1662 *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1662) "Book of Common Prayer (1662)") compulsory; the [Conventicle Act 1664](/wiki/Conventicle_Act_1664 "Conventicle Act 1664") prohibited religious assemblies of more than five people, except under the auspices of the Church of England; and the [Five Mile Act 1665](/wiki/Five_Mile_Act_1665 "Five Mile Act 1665") prohibited expelled non-conforming clergymen from coming within five miles (8 km) of a parish from which they had been banished. The Conventicle and Five Mile Acts remained in effect for the remainder of Charles's reign. The Acts became known as the [Clarendon Code](/wiki/Clarendon_Code "Clarendon Code"), after Lord Clarendon, even though he was not directly responsible for them and even spoke against the Five Mile Act.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989229-38)

The Restoration was accompanied by social change. [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism") lost its momentum. Theatres reopened after having been closed during the [protectorship](/wiki/Interregnum_(England) "Interregnum (England)") of Oliver Cromwell, and bawdy "[Restoration comedy](/wiki/Restoration_comedy "Restoration comedy")" became a recognisable genre. Theatre licences granted by Charles required that female parts be played by "their natural performers", rather than by boys as was often the practice before;[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989185-39) and [Restoration literature](/wiki/Restoration_literature "Restoration literature") celebrated or reacted to the restored court, which included [libertines](/wiki/Libertine "Libertine") such as [Lord Rochester](/wiki/John_Wilmot,_2nd_Earl_of_Rochester "John Wilmot, 2nd Earl of Rochester"). Of Charles II, Rochester supposedly said:

> We have a pretty, witty king,  
> Whose word no man relies on,  
> He never said a foolish thing,  
> And never did a wise one[[36]](#cite_note-40)

To which Charles is reputed to have replied "that the matter was easily accounted for: For that his discourse was his own, his actions were the ministry's".[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHume1778212-41)

### Great Plague and Great Fire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Great Plague and Great Fire")]

In 1665, the [Great Plague of London](/wiki/Great_Plague_of_London "Great Plague of London") began, peaking in September with up to 7,000 deaths per week.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979238-42) Charles, his family, and the court fled London in July to [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury "Salisbury"); Parliament met in [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1991120-43) Plague cases ebbed over the winter, and Charles returned to London in February 1666.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus1972105-44)

After a long spell of hot and dry weather through mid-1666, the [Great Fire of London](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_London "Great Fire of London") started on 2 September 1666 in [Pudding Lane](/wiki/Pudding_Lane "Pudding Lane"). Fanned by strong winds and fed by wood and fuel stockpiled for winter, the fire destroyed about 13,200 houses and 87 churches, including [St Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Paul%27s_Cathedral "St Paul's Cathedral").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorter2007-45) Charles and his brother James joined and directed the firefighting effort. The public blamed Catholic conspirators for the fire.[[42]](#cite_note-46)

## Foreign policy and marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foreign policy and marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/English_School_-_King_Charles_II_and_Catherine_of_Braganza.png/330px-English_School_-_King_Charles_II_and_Catherine_of_Braganza.png)](/wiki/File:English_School_-_King_Charles_II_and_Catherine_of_Braganza.png)

Charles and Catherine

Since 1640, Portugal had been fighting a [war against Spain](/wiki/Portuguese_Restoration_War "Portuguese Restoration War") to restore its independence after a [dynastic union](/wiki/Dynastic_union "Dynastic union") of 60 years between the crowns of Spain and Portugal. Portugal had been helped by France, but in the [Treaty of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Pyrenees "Treaty of the Pyrenees") in 1659 Portugal was abandoned by its French ally. Negotiations with Portugal for Charles's marriage to [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") began during his father's reign. Upon the restoration, [Queen Luísa of Portugal](/wiki/Luisa_de_Guzm%C3%A1n "Luisa de Guzmán"), acting as regent, reopened negotiations with England that resulted in an alliance.[[43]](#cite_note-47) On 23 June 1661, a marriage treaty was signed; England acquired Catherine's [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") of the port of [Tangier](/wiki/Portuguese_Tangier "Portuguese Tangier") in North Africa, the [Seven Islands of Bombay](/wiki/Seven_Islands_of_Bombay "Seven Islands of Bombay") in India (which had a major influence on the development of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire")), valuable trading privileges in Brazil and the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies"), religious and commercial freedom in Portugal, and two million Portuguese crowns (equivalent to £300,000 then[[e]](#cite_note-49)). Portugal obtained military and naval support against Spain and liberty of worship for Catherine.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynne2004-50) Since Philip IV of Spain was an enemy of the Braganzas and was against a marriage between Catherine and any powerful European monarch, the Spanish government proposed Princess [Maria of Orange-Nassau](/wiki/Maria_of_Orange-Nassau "Maria of Orange-Nassau"), the youngest of the Dutch princesses of the House of Orange, as a bride for Charles, instead of Catherine.[[46]](#cite_note-51) If the English king accepted this proposal, then Philip IV would provide the dowry. As an alternative, the Spanish government also proposed the youngest sister of [the Duke of Parma](/wiki/Ranuccio_II_Farnese "Ranuccio II Farnese"), Caterina Farnese, who was said to be very beautiful, and in February 1661 the earl of Bristol travelled to Parma to inspect the princess.[[47]](#cite_note-52) However, she refused to marry the English monarch, because she wanted to become a nun.[[48]](#cite_note-53) Nevertheless, Charles II dismissed the Dutch and Italian princesses offered by the Spanish government, and chose to marry Catherine of Braganza because of her huge dowry. Catherine journeyed from Portugal to [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth "Portsmouth") on 13–14 May 1662,[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynne2004-50) but was not visited by Charles there until 20 May. The next day the couple were married at Portsmouth in two ceremonies—a Catholic one conducted in secret, followed by a public Anglican service.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynne2004-50)

The same year, in an unpopular move, Charles [sold Dunkirk](/wiki/Sale_of_Dunkirk "Sale of Dunkirk") to King Louis XIV of France for about £375,000.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller199193,_99-54) The channel port, although a valuable strategic outpost, was a drain on Charles's limited finances, as it cost the Treasury £321,000 per year.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989184-55)

[![Obverse of medal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/CharlesII1667Medal.jpg/250px-CharlesII1667Medal.jpg)](/wiki/File:CharlesII1667Medal.jpg)

Charles II in profile on a medal struck in 1667 by [John Roettiers](/wiki/John_Roettiers "John Roettiers") to commemorate the [Second Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Dutch_War "Second Dutch War")

Before Charles's restoration, the [Navigation Acts](/wiki/Navigation_Acts "Navigation Acts") of 1650 had hurt [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") trade by giving English vessels a monopoly, and had started the [First Dutch War](/wiki/First_Dutch_War "First Dutch War") (1652–1654). To lay foundations for a new beginning, envoys of the [States General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") appeared in November 1660 with the [Dutch Gift](/wiki/Dutch_Gift "Dutch Gift").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1998749–750-56) The [Second Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Dutch_War "Second Dutch War") (1665–1667) was started by English attempts to muscle in on Dutch possessions in Africa and North America. The conflict began well for the English, with the capture of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") (renamed New York in honour of Charles's brother James, Duke of York) and a victory at the [Battle of Lowestoft](/wiki/Battle_of_Lowestoft "Battle of Lowestoft"), but in 1667 the Dutch launched a surprise attack on England (the [Raid on the Medway](/wiki/Raid_on_the_Medway "Raid on the Medway")) when they sailed up the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") to where a major part of the English fleet was docked. Almost all of the ships were sunk except for the flagship, [*Royal Charles*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Charles_(1660) "HMS Royal Charles (1660)"), which was taken back to the Netherlands as a [prize](/wiki/Prize_(law) "Prize (law)").[[f]](#cite_note-57) The Second Dutch War ended with the signing of the [Treaty of Breda](/wiki/Treaty_of_Breda_(1667) "Treaty of Breda (1667)").

As a result of the Second Dutch War, Charles dismissed Lord Clarendon, whom he used as a scapegoat for the war.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989250–251-58) Clarendon fled to France when impeached for [high treason](/wiki/High_treason "High treason") (which carried the penalty of death). Power passed to five politicians known collectively by a whimsical [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") as the [Cabal](/wiki/Cabal "Cabal")—the [Baron *Clifford*](/wiki/Thomas_Clifford,_1st_Baron_Clifford "Thomas Clifford, 1st Baron Clifford"), [Earl of *Arlington*](/wiki/Henry_Bennet,_1st_Earl_of_Arlington "Henry Bennet, 1st Earl of Arlington"), [Duke of *Buckingham*](/wiki/George_Villiers,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham"), [Baron *Ashley* (afterwards Earl of Shaftesbury)](/wiki/Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury "Anthony Ashley Cooper, 1st Earl of Shaftesbury") and [Duke of *Lauderdale*](/wiki/John_Maitland,_1st_Duke_of_Lauderdale "John Maitland, 1st Duke of Lauderdale"). In fact, the Cabal rarely acted in concert, and the court was often divided between two factions led by Arlington and Buckingham, with Arlington the more successful.[[53]](#cite_note-59)

In 1668, England allied itself with Sweden and with its former enemy the Netherlands to oppose Louis XIV in the [War of Devolution](/wiki/War_of_Devolution "War of Devolution"). Louis made peace with the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1668) "Triple Alliance (1668)"), but he continued to maintain his aggressive intentions towards the Netherlands. In 1670, Charles, seeking to solve his financial troubles, agreed to the [Treaty of Dover](/wiki/Treaty_of_Dover "Treaty of Dover"), under which Louis would pay him £160,000 each year. In exchange, Charles agreed to supply Louis with troops and to announce his conversion to Catholicism "as soon as the welfare of his kingdom will permit".[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979275-60) Louis was to provide him with 6,000 troops to suppress those who opposed the conversion. Charles endeavoured to ensure that the Treaty—especially the conversion clause—remained secret.[[55]](#cite_note-61) It remains unclear whether Charles ever seriously intended to convert.[[56]](#cite_note-62)

Meanwhile, by a series of five charters, Charles granted the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") the rights to autonomous government of its territorial acquisitions, to mint money, to command fortresses and troops, to form alliances, to make war and peace, and to exercise both civil and [criminal jurisdiction](/wiki/Criminal_jurisdiction "Criminal jurisdiction") over its possessions in the Indies.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911835-63) Earlier in 1668 he leased the islands of [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") to the company for a nominal sum of £10 paid in gold.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBritish_Library_Learning-64) The Portuguese territories that Catherine brought with her as a dowry proved too expensive to maintain; [Tangier](/wiki/English_Tangier "English Tangier") was abandoned in 1684.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989426-65) In 1670, Charles granted control of the entire [Hudson Bay](/wiki/Hudson_Bay "Hudson Bay") drainage basin to the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") by royal charter, and named the territory [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land"), after his cousin [Prince Rupert of the Rhine](/wiki/Prince_Rupert_of_the_Rhine "Prince Rupert of the Rhine"), the company's first governor.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Hudson's_Bay_Company''2017-66)

## Conflict with Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conflict with Parliament")]

Although previously favourable to the Crown, the Cavalier Parliament was alienated by the king's wars and religious policies during the 1670s. In 1672, Charles issued the [Royal Declaration of Indulgence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Indulgence_(1672) "Declaration of Indulgence (1672)"), in which he purported to suspend all [penal laws](/wiki/Penal_law_(Britain) "Penal law (Britain)") against Catholics and other religious dissenters. In the same year, he openly supported Catholic France and started the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War").[[61]](#cite_note-67)

The Cavalier Parliament opposed the Declaration of Indulgence on constitutional grounds by claiming that the king had no right to arbitrarily suspend laws passed by Parliament. Charles withdrew the Declaration, and also agreed to the [Test Act](/wiki/Test_Act "Test Act"), which not only required public officials to receive the [sacrament](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") under the forms prescribed by the Church of England,[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaithby1819782–785-68) but also later forced them to denounce [transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") and the Catholic Mass as "superstitious and idolatrous".[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERaithby1819a894–896-69) Clifford, who had converted to Catholicism, resigned rather than take the oath, and died shortly after, possibly from suicide. By 1674, England had gained nothing from the Anglo-Dutch War, and the Cavalier Parliament refused to provide further funds, forcing Charles to make peace. The power of the Cabal waned, and that of Clifford's replacement [Lord Danby](/wiki/Lord_Danby "Lord Danby") grew.

[![Charles accepts a pineapple from a kneeling man in front of a grand country house.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Charles-pineapple.jpg/250px-Charles-pineapple.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles-pineapple.jpg)

*[Charles II Presented with the First Pineapple Grown in England](/wiki/Charles_II_Presented_with_the_First_Pineapple_Grown_in_England "Charles II Presented with the First Pineapple Grown in England")* by [Hendrick Danckerts](/wiki/Hendrick_Danckerts "Hendrick Danckerts"), c. 1675–1680

Queen Catherine was unable to produce an heir; her four pregnancies had ended in [miscarriages](/wiki/Miscarriage "Miscarriage") and [stillbirths](/wiki/Stillbirth "Stillbirth") in 1662, February 1666, May 1668, and June 1669.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir1996255–257-5) Charles's [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive") was therefore his unpopular Catholic brother, James, Duke of York. Partly to assuage public fears that the royal family was too Catholic, Charles agreed that James's daughter [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") should marry the Protestant [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England").[[64]](#cite_note-70) In 1678, [Titus Oates](/wiki/Titus_Oates "Titus Oates"), who had been alternately an Anglican and [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") priest, falsely warned of a "[Popish Plot](/wiki/Popish_Plot "Popish Plot")" to assassinate the king, even accusing the queen of complicity. Charles did not believe the allegations, but ordered his chief minister Lord Danby to investigate. While Danby seems to have been rightly sceptical of Oates's claims, the Cavalier Parliament took them seriously.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989359–362-71) The people were seized with an anti-Catholic hysteria;[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979360-72) judges and juries across the land condemned the supposed conspirators; numerous innocent individuals were executed.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979375-73)

Later in 1678, the House of Commons impeached Danby for [high treason](/wiki/High_treason "High treason"). Although much of the nation had sought war with Catholic France, Charles had secretly negotiated with Louis XIV, trying to reach an agreement under which England would remain neutral in return for money. Danby had publicly professed that he was hostile to France, but had reservedly agreed to abide by Charles's wishes. The House of Commons did not view him as a reluctant participant in the scandal, instead believing that he was the author of the policy. To save Danby from the impeachment trial, Charles dissolved the Cavalier Parliament in January 1679.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1991278,_301–304-74)

The new English Parliament, which met in March of the same year, was quite hostile to Charles. Many members feared that he had intended to use the [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army_(Great_Britain) "Standing army (Great Britain)") to suppress dissent or impose Catholicism. However, with insufficient funds voted by Parliament, Charles was forced to gradually disband his troops. Having lost the support of Parliament, Danby resigned his post of [Lord High Treasurer](/wiki/Lord_High_Treasurer "Lord High Treasurer"), but received a pardon from the king. In defiance of the royal will, the House of Commons declared that the dissolution of Parliament did not interrupt impeachment proceedings, and that the pardon was therefore invalid. When the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") attempted to impose the punishment of exile—which the Commons thought too mild—the impeachment became stalled between the two Houses. As he had been required to do so many times during his reign, Charles bowed to his opponents' wishes, committing Danby to the [Tower of London](/wiki/Tower_of_London "Tower of London"), in which he was held for another five years.[[69]](#cite_note-75)

## Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Science")]

[![Oil portrait of Charles with heavy jowls, a wig of long black curls and in a suit of armour](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/King_Charles_II_of_England_%281630-1685%29.TIF/lossy-page1-250px-King_Charles_II_of_England_%281630-1685%29.TIF.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_II_of_England_(1630-1685).TIF)

Portrait by [John Riley](/wiki/John_Riley_(painter,_born_1646) "John Riley (painter, born 1646)"), c. 1683–1684

In Charles's early childhood, [William Cavendish, Earl of Newcastle](/wiki/William_Cavendish,_Earl_of_Newcastle "William Cavendish, Earl of Newcastle"), was governor of the royal household and Brian Duppa, the [Dean of Christ Church, Oxford](/wiki/Dean_of_Christ_Church,_Oxford "Dean of Christ Church, Oxford"), was his tutor.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAiry19047-76) Neither man thought that the study of science subjects was appropriate for a future king,[[71]](#cite_note-77) and Newcastle even advised against studying any subject too seriously.[[72]](#cite_note-78) However, as Charles grew older, the surgeon [William Harvey](/wiki/William_Harvey "William Harvey") was appointed his tutor.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAiry19047-76)[[73]](#cite_note-Carvalho-79) He was famous for his work on blood circulation in the human body and already held the position of physician to Charles I; his studies were to influence Charles's own attitude to science. As the king's chief physician, Harvey accompanied Charles I to the [Battle of Edgehill](/wiki/Battle_of_Edgehill "Battle of Edgehill") and, although some details are uncertain,[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAiry190415-80)[[75]](#cite_note-81) he had charge of Prince Charles and the Duke of York in the morning,[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser197936-82) but the two boys were back with the king for the start of battle.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScottTurtonvon_Arni200479-83)[[78]](#cite_note-84) Later in the afternoon, with their father concerned for their safety, the two princes left the battlefield accompanied by Sir W. Howard and his pensioners.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScottTurtonvon_Arni2004130-85)

During his exile, in France, Charles continued his education, including physics, chemistry and mathematics.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUglow2009220-86) His tutors included the cleric [John Earle](/wiki/John_Earle_(bishop) "John Earle (bishop)"), well known for his satirical book *Microcosmographie*, with whom he studied Latin and Greek, and [Thomas Hobbes](/wiki/Thomas_Hobbes "Thomas Hobbes"), the philosopher and author of *Leviathan*, with whom he studied mathematics.[[81]](#cite_note-87) In France, Charles assisted his childhood friend, [George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham](/wiki/George_Villiers,_2nd_Duke_of_Buckingham "George Villiers, 2nd Duke of Buckingham"), with his experiments in [chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry") and [alchemy](/wiki/Alchemy "Alchemy"),[[82]](#cite_note-88) with the Earl convinced he was close to producing the [philosopher's stone](/wiki/Philosopher%27s_stone "Philosopher's stone"). Although some of Charles's studies, while abroad, may have helped to pass the time,[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus197230-89) on his return to England he was already knowledgeable in the mathematics of navigation and was a competent chemist.[[84]](#cite_note-90) Such was his knowledge of naval architecture that he was able to participate in technical discussions on the subject with [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys"), [William Petty](/wiki/William_Petty "William Petty") and [John Evelyn](/wiki/John_Evelyn "John Evelyn").[[85]](#cite_note-91)

The new concepts and discoveries being found at this time fascinated Charles,[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanrahan200625-92) not only in science and medicine, but in topics such as botany and gardening.[[73]](#cite_note-Carvalho-79)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus197282-93) A French traveller, Sorbier, while visiting the English court, was astonished by the extent of the king's knowledge.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus1972209-94) The king freely indulged in his many interests, including astronomy, which had been stimulated by a visit to [Gresham College](/wiki/Gresham_College "Gresham College"), in October 1660, to see the [telescopes](/wiki/Telescopes "Telescopes") made by the astronomer [Sir Paul Neile](/wiki/Sir_Paul_Neile "Sir Paul Neile").[[89]](#cite_note-95) Charles was so impressed by what he saw that he ordered his own 36' telescope, which he had installed in the Privy Garden at [Whitehall](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall").[[90]](#cite_note-Wright2000-96) He would invite his friends and acquaintances to view the heavens through his new telescope and, in May 1661, Evelyn describes his visit to the Garden, with several other scientists, to view [Saturn's rings](/wiki/Saturn%27s_rings "Saturn's rings").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvelyn1952357-97) Charles also had a laboratory installed in Whitehall, within easy access to his bedroom.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPepys1906b611-98)[[90]](#cite_note-Wright2000-96)[[93]](#cite_note-Ashley-99)

From the beginning of his reign, Charles appointed experts to assist him in his scientific pursuits. These included: [Timothy Clarke](/wiki/Timothy_Clarke "Timothy Clarke"), a celebrated anatomist, who performed some dissections for the king;[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPepys1906a365-100) [Robert Morison](/wiki/Robert_Morison "Robert Morison") as his chief botanist (Charles had his own botanical garden);[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFalkus197282-93) [Edmund Dickinson](/wiki/Edmund_Dickinson "Edmund Dickinson"), a chemist and alchemist, who was tasked with carrying out experiments in the king's laboratory;[[95]](#cite_note-101)[[96]](#cite_note-102) [Sir Thomas Williams](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Williams,_1st_Baronet "Sir Thomas Williams, 1st Baronet"), who was skillful in compounding and inventing medicines, some of which were prepared in the royal presence;[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruce189096-103) and [Nicasius le Febure](/wiki/Nicasius_le_Febure "Nicasius le Febure") (or Nicolas LeFevre), who was invited to England as royal professor of chemistry and apothecary to the king's household.[[98]](#cite_note-104) Evelyn visited his laboratory with the king.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvelyn1952376-105)

In addition to his many other interests, the king was fascinated by clock mechanisms[[73]](#cite_note-Carvalho-79) and had clocks distributed all around Whitehall, including seven of them in his bedroom.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUglow2009221-106) [Robert Bruce](/wiki/Robert_Bruce,_1st_Earl_of_Ailesbury "Robert Bruce, 1st Earl of Ailesbury") (later Earl of Ailesbury), a Gentleman of the Bedchamber, complained that the continual noise of the clocks chiming disturbed his sleep, whenever it was necessary for him to stay close by to the king.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruce189087-107) Also, Charles had a sundial installed in the Privy Garden,[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUglow2009228-108) by which he could set his personal [pocket watch](/wiki/Pocket_watch "Pocket watch").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979250-109) (For a while, the king personally recorded the performance of the latest spring-balance watch, presented to him by [Robert Hooke](/wiki/Robert_Hooke "Robert Hooke").[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine2004202-110))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/British_School%2C_17th_century_-_Charles_II_%28%5E%29_leaving_Hampton_Court_-_RCIN_402006_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-British_School%2C_17th_century_-_Charles_II_%28%5E%29_leaving_Hampton_Court_-_RCIN_402006_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_School,_17th_century_-_Charles_II_(%5E)_leaving_Hampton_Court_-_RCIN_402006_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

Charles leaving [Hampton Court Palace](/wiki/Hampton_Court_Palace "Hampton Court Palace")

In 1662, Charles was pleased to grant a royal charter to a group of scientists and others who had established a formal society in 1660 to give a more academic and learned approach to science and to conduct experiments in physics and mathematics.[[93]](#cite_note-Ashley-99)[[105]](#cite_note-111) [Sir Robert Moray](/wiki/Sir_Robert_Moray "Sir Robert Moray"), a member of Charles's court, played an important part in achieving this outcome, and he was to be the first president of this new [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society"). Over the years, Moray was an important go-between for Charles and the Society,[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979251-112) and his standing with the king was so high that he was given access to the royal laboratory to perform his own experiments there.[[107]](#cite_note-113)

Charles never attended a Society meeting,[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJardine2004106-114) but he remained aware of the activities there from his discussions with Society members, especially Moray.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUglow2009228-108) In addition, [Robert Boyle](/wiki/Robert_Boyle "Robert Boyle") gave him a private viewing of the Boyle/Hooke [air-pump](/wiki/Air-pump "Air-pump"),[[109]](#cite_note-West-115)[[110]](#cite_note-Nichols-116) which was used at many of the Wednesday meetings. However, Charles preferred experiments that had an immediate practical outcome[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979250-109) and he laughed at the efforts of the Society members "to weigh air".[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPepys1906a451-117) He seemed unable to grasp the significance of the basic laws of physics being established at that time, including [Boyle's law](/wiki/Boyle%27s_law "Boyle's law") and [Hooke's law](/wiki/Hooke%27s_law "Hooke's law") and the concept of atmospheric pressure[[109]](#cite_note-West-115) and the [barometer](/wiki/Barometer "Barometer")[[112]](#cite_note-118) and the importance of air for the support of life.[[110]](#cite_note-Nichols-116)

Although Charles lost interest in the activities of the society, he continued to support scientific and commercial endeavours. He founded the Mathematical School at [Christ's Hospital](/wiki/Christ%27s_Hospital "Christ's Hospital") in 1673 and, two years later, following concerns over French advances in astronomy, he founded the [Royal Observatory](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich") at Greenwich.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUglow2009241–242-119) He maintained an interest in chemistry and regularly visited his private laboratory.[[90]](#cite_note-Wright2000-96)[[93]](#cite_note-Ashley-99) There, dissections observed by the king were occasionally carried out.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUglow2009221-106) Pepys noted in his diary that on the morning of Friday, 15 January 1669, while he was walking to Whitehall, he met the king, who invited him to view his chemistry laboratory. Pepys confessed to finding what he saw there beyond him.[[114]](#cite_note-120)

Charles developed painful [gout](/wiki/Gout "Gout") in later life which limited the daily walks that he took regularly when younger. His keenness was now channelled to his laboratory where he would devote himself to his experiments for hours at a time,[[115]](#cite_note-121)[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979586-122) sometimes helped by Moray.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAiry1904198-123) Charles was particularly interested in alchemy, which he had first encountered many years earlier during his exile with the Duke of Buckingham. Charles resumed his experiments with mercury and would spend whole mornings attempting to distill it. Heating mercury in an open crucible releases mercury vapour, which is toxic and may have contributed to his later ill health.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979567–596-124)[[119]](#cite_note-125)

## Navy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Navy")]

Charles had a great interest in naval affairs.[[g]](#cite_note-127) He regularly attended meetings of the Admiralty Board and was the most frequent participant between January 1674 and April 1679. Samuel Pepys described the management of the navy, between 1673 and 1679, as being "wholly performed by the immediate direction of" the king, with the advice of the [Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") (the future James II). The decisions made by the king alone and those made by the Admiralty Board can be differentiated in Pepys's minutes by his terminology. This makes clear that decision-making on the deployment of warships and the appointment of their commissioned officers was almost exclusively carried out by Charles. Surviving Royal records on the navy contain sailing orders and instructions which are not recorded in the Admiralty minutes, confirming this conclusion.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies201760–61,_108–110-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/1972P53_The_English_Ship_Hampton_Court_in_a_Gale.jpg/250px-1972P53_The_English_Ship_Hampton_Court_in_a_Gale.jpg)](/wiki/File:1972P53_The_English_Ship_Hampton_Court_in_a_Gale.jpg)

[HMS *Hampton Court*](/wiki/HMS_Hampton_Court_(1678) "HMS Hampton Court (1678)"), a third-rate in the thirty ship programme

Charles voluntarily took on more mundane management tasks. He signed all captains' and lieutenants' commissions, and his signature was, for example, on the warrant for Thomas Shish as master shipwright at [Sheerness](/wiki/Sheerness_dockyard "Sheerness dockyard"), with Charles having the final choice, 6 months later, of appointing Shish to the same position in the [Woolwich Dockyard](/wiki/Woolwich_Dockyard "Woolwich Dockyard"). He was present at most of the important ship launches and many minor ones. Since all ship names were decided on by Charles, with no apparent input from anyone else, the name of a new ship usually travelled to the launching ceremony in the king's head.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies201760–61,_108-129)[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEndsor200921–22-130)

Following the political crisis of 1678 the Admiralty Board demanded, in January 1679, that they regain control of some functions carried out by the King. On 10 May, before Charles ultimately conceded, he appointed 88 warrant officers for the new ships being built under the thirty ship programme,[[h]](#cite_note-thirty-132) to ensure that he had his own choice of people in these jobs before the Board took over. Thereafter, he used the royal prerogative to get his way on any other decision with which he disagreed.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies2017113–116-133) An example of Charles's continuing control of operational naval matters is his support for [Arthur Herbert](/wiki/Arthur_Herbert,_1st_Earl_of_Torrington "Arthur Herbert, 1st Earl of Torrington") as commander-in-chief in the Mediterranean from 1679 to 1683. Herbert was widely disliked, and the Admiralty Board and Pepys (who were not normally allies) asked for his dismissal. The King consistently resisted, with the benefit that Herbert's tactic of relying on convoys to protect British ships was shown to have the best result.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies2017116–117-134)

Further political problems had, by 1682, left the Admiralty Board split along political lines, reducing its ability to function. (Pepys was no longer in post.) Charles took advantage of this by creating the "Irish Squadron". This naval force was under his direct administration, with orders going through the Secretary of State, rather than the Admiralty. The squadron was immediately used to supply the English colony in [Tangier](/wiki/Tangier "Tangier"). All the detailed orders for these ships came from Charles with the Secretary of State merely passing them on.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies2017117–119-135)

Charles had a good understanding of [naval architecture](/wiki/Naval_architecture "Naval architecture") and took decisions on the plans for new ships. For instance, among the interventions he made to the thirty ship programme,[[h]](#cite_note-thirty-132) he pointed out that with the French as a likely enemy in future wars, English warships would need to spend longer at sea than when fighting the Dutch.[[i]](#cite_note-137) Consequently, Charles insisted on the [third-rates](/wiki/Third-rate "Third-rate") being built larger than the minimum size laid down by parliament. This increased the capacity of stores, but also made the ships more seaworthy and better gun platforms. He guaranteed to pay the extra costs from his own funds, but with the ships built, he then reneged on this promise. Charles also instructed that ships in the programme should be launched as soon as the hulls were ready, with remaining work to be done afloat. This freed up docks and slipways for the next ship to be started, so helping speed up the ambitious construction timetable.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEndsor200911-13-138)[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies201775–77-139)

Not all Charles's efforts in naval architecture were successful. When launched, the experimental [HMS *Royal Katherine*](/wiki/HMS_Royal_Katherine "HMS Royal Katherine") needed immediate remedial work[[j]](#cite_note-142) to correct her instability. Charles supported a suggestion by Pepys that the draught of ships in the thirty ship programme could be reduced. This was strongly resisted by the shipwrights, and the King ultimately deferred to their expert opinion.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies201776–77-140)

Charles learnt to sail when he had fled the Royalist defeat in the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). Taking refuge in [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") in 1646, he was provided with a [pinnace](/wiki/Pinnace_(ship%27s_boat) "Pinnace (ship's boat)") for his amusement. He regularly took the helm and was under the tuition of Royalist naval officers who had similarly left England. After the Restoration, Charles had a number of [Royal Yachts](/wiki/List_of_royal_yachts_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of royal yachts of the United Kingdom") and frequently chose to make use of them.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies20179,_38-143)

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Later years")]

Charles faced a political storm over his brother James, a Catholic, being next in line to the throne. The prospect of a Catholic monarch was vehemently opposed by the [1st Earl of Shaftesbury](/wiki/1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury "1st Earl of Shaftesbury") (a former member of the Cabal, which had fallen apart in 1673). Lord Shaftesbury's power base was strengthened when the House of Commons of 1679 introduced the [Exclusion Bill](/wiki/Exclusion_Bill "Exclusion Bill"), which sought to exclude the Duke of York from the [line of succession](/wiki/Succession_to_the_British_throne "Succession to the British throne"). Some even sought to confer the Crown on the Protestant [Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/Duke_of_Monmouth "Duke of Monmouth"), the eldest of Charles's illegitimate children. The *Abhorrers*—those who thought the Exclusion Bill was abhorrent—were named [Tories](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") (after a term for dispossessed Irish Catholic bandits), while the *Petitioners*—those who supported a petitioning campaign in favour of the Exclusion Bill—were called [Whigs](/wiki/British_Whig_Party "British Whig Party") (after a term for rebellious Scottish Presbyterians).[[134]](#cite_note-144)

### Absolute monarch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Absolute monarch")]

Fearing that the Exclusion Bill would be passed, and bolstered by some acquittals in the continuing Plot trials, which seemed to him to indicate a more favourable public mood towards Catholicism, Charles dissolved the English Parliament, for a second time that year, in mid-1679. Charles's hopes for a more moderate Parliament were not fulfilled; within a few months he had dissolved Parliament yet again, after it sought to pass the Exclusion Bill. When a new Parliament assembled at Oxford in March 1681, Charles dissolved it for a fourth time after just a few days.[[135]](#cite_note-145) During the 1680s, however, popular support for the Exclusion Bill ebbed, and Charles experienced a nationwide surge of loyalty. Lord Shaftesbury was prosecuted (albeit unsuccessfully) for treason in 1681 and later fled to Holland, where he died. For the remainder of his reign, Charles ruled without Parliament.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989430–441-146)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Charles_II_touching_the_scrofulous_%28crop%29.jpeg/250px-Charles_II_touching_the_scrofulous_%28crop%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_touching_the_scrofulous_(crop).jpeg)

Charles performing the [royal touch](/wiki/Royal_touch "Royal touch"); engraving by [Robert White](/wiki/Robert_White_(engraver) "Robert White (engraver)"), 1684

Charles's opposition to the Exclusion Bill angered some Protestants. Protestant conspirators formulated the [Rye House Plot](/wiki/Rye_House_Plot "Rye House Plot"), a plan to murder him and the Duke of York as they returned to London after horse races in [Newmarket](/wiki/Newmarket,_Suffolk "Newmarket, Suffolk"). A great fire, however, destroyed [Charles's lodgings at Newmarket](/wiki/Charles%27s_lodgings_at_Newmarket "Charles's lodgings at Newmarket"), which forced him to leave the races early, thus inadvertently avoiding the planned attack. News of the failed plot was leaked.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979426-147) Protestant politicians such as the [Earl of Essex](/wiki/Arthur_Capell,_1st_Earl_of_Essex "Arthur Capell, 1st Earl of Essex"), [Algernon Sydney](/wiki/Algernon_Sydney "Algernon Sydney"), [Lord Russell](/wiki/William_Russell,_Lord_Russell "William Russell, Lord Russell") and the Duke of Monmouth were implicated in the plot. Essex slit his own throat while imprisoned in the Tower of London; Sydney and Russell were executed for high treason on very flimsy evidence; and the Duke of Monmouth went into exile at the court of William of Orange. Lord Danby and the surviving Catholic lords held in the Tower were released and the king's Catholic brother, James, acquired greater influence at court.[[138]](#cite_note-148) Titus Oates was convicted and imprisoned for defamation.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979437-149)

Thus through the last years of Charles's reign, his approach towards his opponents changed, and he was compared by Whigs to the contemporary Louis XIV of France, with his form of government in those years termed "slavery". Many of them were prosecuted and their estates seized, with Charles replacing judges and sheriffs at will and packing juries to achieve conviction. To destroy opposition in London, Charles first disenfranchised many Whigs in the 1682 municipal elections, and in 1683 the [London charter](/wiki/Ancient_borough#Charters_of_incorporation "Ancient borough") was forfeited. In retrospect, the use of the judicial system by Charles (and later his brother and heir James) as a tool against opposition helped establish the idea of [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers") between the judiciary and the Crown in Whig thought.[[140]](#cite_note-150)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Death")]

Charles suffered a sudden [apoplectic fit](/wiki/Apoplectic_fit "Apoplectic fit") on the morning of 2 February 1685, and died four days later at the [Palace of Whitehall](/wiki/Palace_of_Whitehall "Palace of Whitehall"), at 11:45 am, aged 54.[[141]](#cite_note-151) The suddenness of his illness and death led to suspicion of poison in the minds of many, including one of the royal doctors, but a more modern medical analysis has held that the symptoms of his final illness are similar to those of [uraemia](/wiki/Uraemia "Uraemia"), a clinical syndrome due to kidney dysfunction.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBMJ1938-152) Charles had a laboratory among his many interests where, prior to his illness, he had been experimenting with [mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(element) "Mercury (element)"). Mercuric poisoning can produce irreversible kidney damage, but the case for that being a cause of his death is unproven.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979586–587-153) In the days between his collapse and his death, Charles endured a variety of torturous treatments, including [bloodletting](/wiki/Bloodletting "Bloodletting"), [purging](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative") and [cupping](/wiki/Cupping_therapy "Cupping therapy"), in the hope of effecting a recovery,[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2015-154) which may have exacerbated his uraemia through dehydration, rather than helping to alleviate it.[[145]](#cite_note-155)

On his deathbed, Charles asked his brother, James, to look after his mistresses: "be well to [Portsmouth](/wiki/Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille,_Duchess_of_Portsmouth "Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth"), and let not poor [Nelly](/wiki/Nell_Gwyn "Nell Gwyn") starve".[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979456-156) He told his courtiers, "I am sorry, gentlemen, for being such a time a-dying",[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant200173-157) and expressed regret at his treatment of his wife. On the last evening of his life he was received into the Catholic Church, in the presence of Father [John Huddleston](/wiki/John_Huddleston "John Huddleston"), though the extent to which he was fully conscious or committed, and with whom the idea originated, is unclear.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989443,_456-158) He was buried in [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") "without any manner of pomp"[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant200173-157) on 14 February.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979459-159)

Charles was succeeded by his brother James II and VII.[[150]](#cite_note-160)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![Lead equestrian statue](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Charles_II_statue._Parliament_Square_Edinburgh.JPG/250px-Charles_II_statue._Parliament_Square_Edinburgh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Charles_II_statue._Parliament_Square_Edinburgh.JPG)

Statue of Charles II as a Roman Caesar, erected 1685, [Parliament Square, Edinburgh](/wiki/Parliament_Square,_Edinburgh "Parliament Square, Edinburgh")

His encouragement of theatre, horse racing, and traditional celebrations included the revival of public Christmas festivities after the Puritan rule of [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") during the English Interregnum. His reputation for [hedonism](/wiki/Hedonism "Hedonism") led later commentators to nickname him the "Merry Monarch" and for his reign to be described as a "Golden Age". However, his biographer [Antonia Fraser](/wiki/Antonia_Fraser "Antonia Fraser") has written that "Merry Monarch" was not appropriate, as Charles was, at heart, marked by melancholy.[[151]](#cite_note-161)

Charles delighted and bored listeners with tales of his escape after his defeat at the [Battle of Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") for many years. Numerous accounts of his adventures were published, particularly in the immediate aftermath of the Restoration. Though not averse to his escape being ascribed to divine providence, Charles himself seems to have delighted most in his ability to sustain his disguise as a man of ordinary origins, and to move unrecognised through his realm. Ironic and cynical, Charles took pleasure in stories that demonstrated the undetectable nature of any inherent majesty he possessed.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeber1988492–493,_505–506-162)

Charles had no legitimate children, but acknowledged at least twelve by seven mistresses,[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979411-163) including five by [Barbara Villiers, Lady Castlemaine](/wiki/Barbara_Palmer,_1st_Duchess_of_Cleveland "Barbara Palmer, 1st Duchess of Cleveland"), for whom the [Dukedom of Cleveland](/wiki/Dukedom_of_Cleveland "Dukedom of Cleveland") was created. His other mistresses included [Moll Davis](/wiki/Moll_Davis "Moll Davis"), [Nell Gwyn](/wiki/Nell_Gwyn "Nell Gwyn"), [Elizabeth Killigrew](/wiki/Elizabeth_Killigrew,_Viscountess_Shannon "Elizabeth Killigrew, Viscountess Shannon"), [Catherine Pegge](/wiki/Catherine_Pegge "Catherine Pegge"), [Lucy Walter](/wiki/Lucy_Walter "Lucy Walter") and [Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth](/wiki/Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille,_Duchess_of_Portsmouth "Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth"). As a result, in his lifetime he was often nicknamed "[Old Rowley](/wiki/Old_Rowley "Old Rowley")", the name of his favourite racehorse, notable as a stallion.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPearson1960147-164)

Charles's subjects resented paying taxes that were spent on his mistresses and their children,[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989338-165) many of whom received dukedoms or earldoms. The present [Dukes of Buccleuch](/wiki/Dukes_of_Buccleuch "Dukes of Buccleuch"), [Richmond](/wiki/Duke_of_Richmond "Duke of Richmond"), [Grafton](/wiki/Duke_of_Grafton "Duke of Grafton") and [St Albans](/wiki/Duke_of_St_Albans "Duke of St Albans") descend from Charles in unbroken male line.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979413-166) Charles II is an ancestor of both King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")'s first wife, [Diana Spencer](/wiki/Diana_Spencer "Diana Spencer"),[[k]](#cite_note-167) and his second wife, [Queen Camilla](/wiki/Queen_Camilla "Queen Camilla"). Charles and Diana's son, [William, Prince of Wales](/wiki/William,_Prince_of_Wales "William, Prince of Wales"), is likely to be the first British monarch descended from Charles II.[[157]](#cite_note-williamson-168)

Charles's eldest son, the [Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/Duke_of_Monmouth "Duke of Monmouth"), led a rebellion against James II, but was defeated at the [Battle of Sedgemoor](/wiki/Battle_of_Sedgemoor "Battle of Sedgemoor") on 6 July 1685, captured and executed. James was eventually dethroned in 1688, in the course of the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution").

[![Gilt statue](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Rhc-charles2.jpg/250px-Rhc-charles2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhc-charles2.jpg)

Statue of Charles II (c. 1682) in ancient Roman dress by [Grinling Gibbons](/wiki/Grinling_Gibbons "Grinling Gibbons") at the [Royal Hospital Chelsea](/wiki/Royal_Hospital_Chelsea "Royal Hospital Chelsea")

In the words of his contemporary [John Evelyn](/wiki/John_Evelyn "John Evelyn"), "a prince of many virtues and many great imperfections, debonair, easy of access, not bloody or cruel".[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1991382–383-169) [John Wilmot, 2nd Earl of Rochester](/wiki/John_Wilmot,_2nd_Earl_of_Rochester "John Wilmot, 2nd Earl of Rochester"), wrote of Charles:

> Restless he rolls from whore to whore  
> A merry monarch, scandalous and poor.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller199195-170)

Looking back on Charles's reign, Tories tended to view it as a time of benevolent monarchy whereas Whigs perceived it as a terrible [despotism](/wiki/Despotism "Despotism"). Professor [Ronald Hutton](/wiki/Ronald_Hutton "Ronald Hutton") summarises a polarised historiography:

> For the past hundred years, books on Charles II have been sharply divided into two categories. Academic historians have concentrated mainly on his activities as a statesman and emphasised his duplicity, self-indulgence, poor judgement and lack of an aptitude for business or for stable and trustworthy government. Non-academic authors have concentrated mainly on his social and cultural world, emphasising his charm, affability, worldliness, tolerance, turning him into one of the most popular of all English monarchs in novels, plays and films.[[160]](#cite_note-171)

Hutton says Charles was a popular king in his own day and a "legendary figure" in British history.

> Other kings had inspired more respect, but perhaps only [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") had endeared himself to the popular imagination as much as this one. He was the playboy monarch, naughty but nice, the hero of all who prized urbanity, tolerance, good humour, and the pursuit of pleasure above the more earnest, sober, or material virtues.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989446-172)

The anniversary of the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") (which was also Charles's birthday)—29 May—was recognised in England until the mid-nineteenth century as [Oak Apple Day](/wiki/Oak_Apple_Day "Oak Apple Day"), after the Royal Oak in which Charles hid during his escape from the forces of Oliver Cromwell. Traditional celebrations involved the wearing of oak leaves but these have now died out.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979118-173) Charles II is [depicted extensively in art, literature and media](/wiki/Cultural_depictions_of_Charles_II_of_England "Cultural depictions of Charles II of England"). [Charleston, South Carolina](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina"), and [South Kingstown, Rhode Island](/wiki/South_Kingstown,_Rhode_Island "South Kingstown, Rhode Island"), are named after him. King Charles's Island and Charles Island are previous names of both [Floreana Island](/wiki/Floreana_Island "Floreana Island") and [Española Island](/wiki/Espa%C3%B1ola_Island "Española Island") in the [Galapagos Archipelago](/wiki/Galapagos_Archipelago "Galapagos Archipelago"), both in his honour.

## Titles, styles, honours and arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Titles, styles, honours and arms")]

### Titles and styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Titles and styles")]
