The official [style](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") of Charles II as king (after 1649) was "Charles the Second, by the Grace of God, [King of England](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_England "List of monarchs of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/List_of_Monarchs_of_Scotland "List of Monarchs of Scotland"), [France](/wiki/English_Kings_of_France "English Kings of France") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), [Defender of the Faith](/wiki/Defender_of_the_Faith "Defender of the Faith"), etc."[[163]](#cite_note-174) The [claim to France](/wiki/English_claims_to_the_French_throne "English claims to the French throne") was only nominal, and had been asserted by every English monarch since [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III"), regardless of the amount of French territory actually controlled.

### Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Honours")]

* **KG**: [Knight of the Garter](/wiki/Knight_of_the_Garter "Knight of the Garter"), 21 May 1638[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir1996255–257-5)

### Arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Arms")]

Charles's [coat of arms as Prince of Wales](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_the_Prince_of_Wales "Coat of arms of the Prince of Wales") was the [royal arms](/wiki/Royal_arms "Royal arms") (which he later inherited), differenced by a [label](/wiki/Label_(heraldry) "Label (heraldry)") of three points [Argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent").[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshmole1715534-175) His arms as monarch were: [Quarterly](/wiki/Quartering_(heraldry) "Quartering (heraldry)"), I and IV Grandquarterly, [Azure](/wiki/Azure_(heraldry) "Azure (heraldry)") three [fleurs-de-lis](/wiki/Fleurs-de-lis "Fleurs-de-lis") [Or](/wiki/Or_(heraldry) "Or (heraldry)") (for France) and [Gules](/wiki/Gules "Gules") three lions [passant guardant](/wiki/Passant_guardant "Passant guardant") in [pale](/wiki/Pale_(heraldry) "Pale (heraldry)") Or ([for England](/wiki/Royal_Arms_of_England "Royal Arms of England")); II Or a lion [rampant](/wiki/Rampant "Rampant") within a double [tressure](/wiki/Tressure "Tressure") flory-counter-flory Gules ([for Scotland](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_Scotland "Royal coat of arms of Scotland")); III Azure a harp Or stringed Argent ([for Ireland](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Ireland "Coat of arms of Ireland")).

## Issue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Issue")]

By [Lucy Walter](/wiki/Lucy_Walter "Lucy Walter") (c. 1630 – 1658):

* [James Crofts, later Scott](/wiki/James_Scott,_1st_Duke_of_Monmouth "James Scott, 1st Duke of Monmouth") (1649–1685), created [Duke of Monmouth](/wiki/Duke_of_Monmouth "Duke of Monmouth") (1663) in England and [Duke of Buccleuch](/wiki/Duke_of_Buccleuch "Duke of Buccleuch") (1663) in Scotland. Monmouth was born nine months after Walter and Charles II first met, and was acknowledged as his son by Charles II, but James II suggested that he was the son of another of her lovers, Colonel Robert Sidney, rather than Charles. Lucy Walter had a daughter, Mary Crofts, born after James in 1651, but Charles II was not the father, since he and Walter parted in September 1649.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeir1996255–257-5)

By [Elizabeth Killigrew](/wiki/Elizabeth_Killigrew,_Viscountess_Shannon "Elizabeth Killigrew, Viscountess Shannon") (1622–1680), daughter of Sir [Robert Killigrew](/wiki/Robert_Killigrew "Robert Killigrew") and wife of [Francis Boyle, 1st Viscount Shannon](/wiki/Francis_Boyle,_1st_Viscount_Shannon "Francis Boyle, 1st Viscount Shannon"):

* [Charlotte Jemima Henrietta Maria FitzRoy](/wiki/Charlotte_Jemima_Henrietta_Maria_FitzRoy "Charlotte Jemima Henrietta Maria FitzRoy") (1650–1684), married firstly [James Howard](/wiki/James_Howard_(dramatist) "James Howard (dramatist)") and secondly [William Paston, 2nd Earl of Yarmouth](/wiki/William_Paston,_2nd_Earl_of_Yarmouth "William Paston, 2nd Earl of Yarmouth")

By [Catherine Pegge](/wiki/Catherine_Pegge "Catherine Pegge"):

* [Charles FitzCharles](/wiki/Charles_FitzCharles,_1st_Earl_of_Plymouth "Charles FitzCharles, 1st Earl of Plymouth") (1657–1680), known as "Don Carlo", created [Earl of Plymouth](/wiki/Earl_of_Plymouth "Earl of Plymouth") (1675)
* [Catherine FitzCharles](/wiki/Catherine_FitzCharles "Catherine FitzCharles") (born 1658; she either died young or became a nun at Dunkirk)[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton1989125-176)

By [Barbara Villiers](/wiki/Barbara_Villiers "Barbara Villiers") (1641–1709), wife of [Roger Palmer, 1st Earl of Castlemaine](/wiki/Roger_Palmer,_1st_Earl_of_Castlemaine "Roger Palmer, 1st Earl of Castlemaine"), and created [Duchess of Cleveland](/wiki/Duke_of_Cleveland "Duke of Cleveland") in her own right:

* [Lady Anne Palmer (Fitzroy)](/wiki/Anne_Lennard,_Countess_of_Sussex "Anne Lennard, Countess of Sussex") (1661–1722), married [Thomas Lennard, 1st Earl of Sussex](/wiki/Thomas_Lennard,_1st_Earl_of_Sussex "Thomas Lennard, 1st Earl of Sussex"). She may have been the daughter of Roger Palmer, but Charles accepted her.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECokayne1926706–708-177)
* [Charles Fitzroy](/wiki/Charles_FitzRoy,_2nd_Duke_of_Cleveland "Charles FitzRoy, 2nd Duke of Cleveland") (1662–1730), created [Duke of Southampton](/wiki/Duke_of_Southampton "Duke of Southampton") (1675), became 2nd [Duke of Cleveland](/wiki/Duke_of_Cleveland "Duke of Cleveland") (1709)
* [Henry Fitzroy](/wiki/Henry_FitzRoy,_1st_Duke_of_Grafton "Henry FitzRoy, 1st Duke of Grafton") (1663–1690), created [Earl of Euston](/wiki/Earl_of_Euston "Earl of Euston") (1672), [Duke of Grafton](/wiki/Duke_of_Grafton "Duke of Grafton") (1675)
* [Charlotte Fitzroy](/wiki/Charlotte_Lee,_Countess_of_Lichfield "Charlotte Lee, Countess of Lichfield") (1664–1717), married [Edward Lee, 1st Earl of Lichfield](/wiki/Edward_Lee,_1st_Earl_of_Lichfield "Edward Lee, 1st Earl of Lichfield")
* [George Fitzroy](/wiki/George_FitzRoy,_1st_Duke_of_Northumberland "George FitzRoy, 1st Duke of Northumberland") (1665–1716), created [Earl of Northumberland](/wiki/Earl_of_Northumberland "Earl of Northumberland") (1674), [Duke of Northumberland](/wiki/Duke_of_Northumberland "Duke of Northumberland") (1678)
* [Barbara (Benedicta) Fitzroy](/wiki/Lady_Barbara_FitzRoy "Lady Barbara FitzRoy") (1672–1737) – She was probably the child of [John Churchill](/wiki/John_Churchill "John Churchill"), later [Duke of Marlborough](/wiki/Dukes_of_Marlborough "Dukes of Marlborough"), who was another of Cleveland's many lovers,[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller199197,_123-178) and was never acknowledged by Charles as his own daughter.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser197965,_286-179)

By [Nell Gwyn](/wiki/Nell_Gwyn "Nell Gwyn") (1650–1687):

* [Charles Beauclerk](/wiki/Charles_Beauclerk,_1st_Duke_of_St_Albans "Charles Beauclerk, 1st Duke of St Albans") (1670–1726), created [Duke of St Albans](/wiki/Duke_of_St_Albans "Duke of St Albans") (1684)
* James, Lord Beauclerk (1671–1680)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Mignard%2C_Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille.jpg/250px-Mignard%2C_Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mignard,_Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille.jpg)

Louise de Kérouaille with unknown attendant, painted in France by [Pierre Mignard](/wiki/Pierre_Mignard "Pierre Mignard"), 1682[[169]](#cite_note-180)

By [Louise Renée de Penancoët de Kérouaille](/wiki/Louise_de_K%C3%A9rouaille,_Duchess_of_Portsmouth "Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth") (1649–1734), created [Duchess of Portsmouth](/wiki/Duchess_of_Portsmouth "Duchess of Portsmouth") in her own right (1673):

* [Charles Lennox](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_1st_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 1st Duke of Richmond") (1672–1723), created [Duke of Richmond](/wiki/Duke_of_Richmond "Duke of Richmond") (1675) in England and [Duke of Lennox](/wiki/Duke_of_Lennox "Duke of Lennox") (1675) in Scotland.

By [Mary 'Moll' Davis](/wiki/Moll_Davis "Moll Davis"), courtesan and actress of repute:[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979287-181)

* [Lady Mary Tudor](/wiki/Lady_Mary_Tudor "Lady Mary Tudor") (1673–1726), married [Edward Radclyffe, 2nd Earl of Derwentwater](/wiki/Edward_Radclyffe,_2nd_Earl_of_Derwentwater "Edward Radclyffe, 2nd Earl of Derwentwater"); after Edward's death, she married [Henry Graham (of Levens)](/wiki/Henry_Graham_(of_Levens) "Henry Graham (of Levens)"), and upon his death she married James Rooke.

Other probable mistresses include:

* Christabella Wyndham[[171]](#cite_note-182)
* [Hortense Mancini](/wiki/Hortense_Mancini "Hortense Mancini"), Duchess of Mazarin[[172]](#cite_note-183)
* [Winifred Wells](/wiki/Winifred_Wells "Winifred Wells") – one of Queen Catherine's Maids of Honour[[173]](#cite_note-mrs-184)
* Jane Roberts – the daughter of a clergyman[[173]](#cite_note-mrs-184)
* Mrs Knight – a famous singer[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBBC_staff2003-185)
* [Elizabeth, Countess of Falmouth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_Countess_of_Falmouth "Elizabeth, Countess of Falmouth") – the widow of [Charles Berkeley, 1st Earl of Falmouth](/wiki/Charles_Berkeley,_1st_Earl_of_Falmouth "Charles Berkeley, 1st Earl of Falmouth")[[173]](#cite_note-mrs-184)[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMelville200591-186)
* Elizabeth Fitzgerald, [Countess of Kildare](/wiki/Earl_of_Kildare "Earl of Kildare")[[173]](#cite_note-mrs-184)

Letters claiming that Marguerite or Margaret de Carteret bore Charles a son named [James de la Cloche](/wiki/James_de_la_Cloche "James de la Cloche") in 1646 are dismissed by historians as forgeries.[[176]](#cite_note-187)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charles_II_of_England&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-reign_1-0)** The traditional date of the Restoration marking the first assembly of King and Parliament together since the abolition of the English monarchy in 1649. The English Parliament recognised Charles as king by unanimous vote on 2 May 1660, and he was proclaimed king in London on 8 May, although royalists had recognised him as such since the execution of his father on 30 January 1649. During Charles's reign all legal documents stating a [regnal year](/wiki/Regnal_year "Regnal year") did so as if his reign began at his father's death.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** From the death of his father to his defeat at the [Battle of Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester")
3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** All dates in this article unless otherwise noted are given in the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") with the start of year adjusted to 1 January (see [Old Style and New Style dates](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates")).
4. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** One thousand pounds was a vast sum at the time, greater than an average workman's lifetime earnings.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFraser1979117-17)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-49)** Equivalent to between £42.7 million (real cost) and £12.7 billion (economic share) as of 2021.[[44]](#cite_note-48)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-57)** The ship's [transom](/wiki/Transom_(nautical) "Transom (nautical)") is on display at the [Rijksmuseum](/wiki/Rijksmuseum "Rijksmuseum") in Amsterdam.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-127)** The navy of the time consumed about a third of England's tax revenue in peacetime. That rose to about 50% in time of war. The dockyards were the biggest industrial complexes in the country.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies201713-126)
8. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-thirty_132-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-thirty_132-1) The thirty ship programme was authorised by parliament on 23 February 1677. It was to address the realisation that the English navy was smaller than that of either the French or the Dutch. One [first-rate](/wiki/First-rate "First-rate") of 1,400 tons, nine second-rates, each of 1,100 tons, and 20 [third-rates](/wiki/Third-rate "Third-rate"), each of not less than 900 tons, were approved.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEndsor200910–11-131)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-137)** If France were the opponent, naval campaigns could be expected to range from the [Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") to the Mediterranean. Combat against the Dutch would likely be in the southern part of the North Sea and the eastern end of the Channel.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEndsor200911-136)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-142)** The process involved was girdling, the addition of an extra layer of planking below the waterline to provide extra breadth to the hull and therefore make the ship more stable.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDavies201776–77-140)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams2013130-141)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-167)** Diana was descended from two of Charles II's illegitimate sons: the [Dukes of Grafton](/wiki/Henry_FitzRoy,_1st_Duke_of_Grafton "Henry FitzRoy, 1st Duke of Grafton") and [Richmond](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_1st_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 1st Duke of Richmond").
